message.content: {"top_matches":[{"player":"cat-pink-8143","probability":0.65},{"player":"dog-orange-384","probability":0.25},{"player":"buffalo-red-6989","probability":0.10}],"behavioral_basis":["cat-pink-8143 repeatedly proposed deals that cost the proposer (large RED offers for fewer GREENs) and ultimately accepted a loss to enable another player’s gain — consistent with overpaying partners and sacrificing personal earnings to produce fair-looking outcomes.","dog-orange-384 accepted a modest positive payoff (+$0.20) and avoided risky or large losses, matching Irene’s tendency to accept modest offers and avoid gambles, but their own proposals were more self-favoring than Irene would produce.","buffalo-red-6989 made low-cost, self-beneficial proposals and never conceded or accepted deals, which contrasts with Irene’s fairness-first, concessionary pattern."],"uncertainty_notes":["Observed actions are few (only 3 rounds) and proposals can be strategic signals rather than stable preferences.","Private chip values differ across players, so some generous-looking offers may be utility-neutral given private valuations; inferring motives from offers is noisy.","Random selection when multiple acceptances can mask intent; there were very few executed trades to observe concession behavior directly."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"camel-pink-767","probability":0.65},{"player":"alligator-orange-9612","probability":0.30},{"player":"butterfly-yellow-2139","probability":0.05}],"behavioral_basis":["Consistent use of even 1:1/2:2/6:6 proposals (camel-pink-767) matches Irene's tendency to frame offers as 'even splits'.","Accepted modest offers and made/accepted small, low‑risk trades (camel and alligator), consistent with Irene's avoidance of risky gambles and willingness to accept modest outcomes.","Both camel-pink-767 and alligator-orange-9612 declined butterfly-yellow-2139's large/one‑sided proposals, which aligns with Irene's tendency to punish blatant selfishness by refusing unfair offers."],"uncertainty_notes":["Observed actions are limited (three rounds) and players' private valuations affect incentives — some \"even\" proposals may have been self‑serving given private values, so similarity to Irene (who sacrifices earnings for fairness) is inferential.","Irene explicitly would overpay partners to equalize payoffs; neither player perfectly demonstrates costly equalizing behavior in the log (final payouts remain unequal), so match probabilities are probabilistic rather than definitive.","Some behaviors (e.g., accepting or proposing symmetric trades) are compatible with multiple motives (fairness, self‑interest, low risk), making attribution uncertain."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"iguana-orange-9224","probability":0.75},{"player":"dolphin-purple-6605","probability":0.20},{"player":"ram-red-5745","probability":0.05}],"behavioral_basis":["Accepted modest offers from ram-red-5745 (small gains rather than maximizing earnings).","Accepted a trade that reduced their own payout (willingness to concede/overpay to rebalance others).","Made proposals that occasionally looked like attempts to redistribute value (offering low-value RED to obtain GREEN and later offering GREEN for RED), consistent with a fairness-oriented, equality-seeking framing."],"uncertainty_notes":["Limited number of observed turns (9 proposal turns) — behavior might shift with more rounds.","Private valuations are known here but motivations still inferred from actions; some actions (e.g., offering low-value chips) can be interpreted multiple ways (strategic vs. fairness).","Random selection when multiple acceptances can obscure intended partner-targeting and altruistic intent."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"goose-purple-9985","probability":0.84},{"player":"buffalo-orange-6610","probability":0.10},{"player":"monkey-pink-6874","probability":0.06}],"behavioral_basis":["goose repeatedly proposed symmetric 2-for-2 trades and framed offers around even splits—directly matches Irene’s emphasis on fairness and even outcomes","goose accepted modest offers from others and even executed a trade that reduced their own payout (round 2), consistent with conceding to rebalance others’ payoffs","buffalo made asymmetric, self-benefiting proposals (2R for 3G) and mostly accepted small profitable trades—partially cooperative but more self-interested than Irene","monkey regularly accepted generous offers that overpaid them and proposed aggressive, high-ask trades that were declined—opportunistic behavior inconsistent with Irene’s fairness-first motive"],"uncertainty_notes":["Only three rounds observed; limited data may not capture longer-run fairness behavior","Players’ private valuations drive incentives; some actions may reflect private-value exploitation rather than stable social preferences","Random selection among multiple acceptors and occasional declines introduce noise in inferring intent"]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"otter-pink-5988","probability":0.75},{"player":"ram-pink-8623","probability":0.20},{"player":"squid-yellow-4597","probability":0.05}],"behavioral_basis":["Otter repeatedly proposes even-looking splits (e.g., 5 GREEN for 5 RED twice), consistent with Irene's tendency to frame around 'even splits' and produce psychologically fair outcomes.","Otter accepted a mildly unfavorable offer (accepted 2 GREEN for 1 RED, incurring a small loss of $0.11), matching Irene's willingness to accept modest offers and concede to rebalance payoffs.","Otter generally avoids large risky gambles (declined some proposers' offers) and repeatedly overpays relative to strict self-interest to achieve apparent equity.","Ram sometimes accepts fair/even proposals (accepted otter's 5G/5R trades) which aligns with cooperative expectations, but also accepted a highly exploitative trade that incurred a large loss, indicating inconsistent risk behavior unlike Irene.","Squid's proposals are larger and more imbalanced (e.g., offering 4 RED to buy 10 GREEN), and squid often declines others' offers—patterns suggest opportunistic or self-interested play rather than Irene's fairness orientation."],"uncertainty_notes":["Behavioral inference is based on three rounds of observed moves; sample size is small and motives can be ambiguous.","Private valuations influence accept/decline decisions, so some acceptances/declines may reflect personal payoff calculus rather than fairness preferences.","Random selection when multiple acceptors and unobserved off-path reasoning (e.g., expectations about reciprocity) add noise to inferred personality."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"turtle-red-9966","probability":0.65},{"player":"dog-green-8079","probability":0.25},{"player":"parrot-pink-5400","probability":0.10}],"behavioral_basis":["turtle-red-9966: Repeatedly accepted proposals that reduced their own payout (conceding to rebalance/pay others), and declined blatantly disadvantageous offers from others — aligns with accepting modest offers and tolerating losses to maintain perceived fairness.","dog-green-8079: Made small, low-risk proposals that repeatedly benefited themselves and accepted modest partner offers — shares Irene's risk-avoidance and acceptance of modest gains but displays more self-serving bargaining than pure equality focus.","parrot-pink-5400: Framed at least one even-looking trade (10 GREEN for 10 RED) but exploited valuation differences to profit; behavior is more self-interested than Irene's stated fairness orientation."],"uncertainty_notes":["Only actions (proposals/accepts/declines) and payouts are observed; private valuations and intentions are inferred and may mask motives.","Small number of rounds — a few concessionary acceptances (especially by turtle) strongly influence the match but could reflect other factors (e.g., mistake, reciprocity toward a specific player).","Players may strategically accept losses to build future leverage or due to misestimation of values; that could confound mapping to a stable fairness-motivated persona."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"buffalo-purple-4069","probability":0.55},{"player":"alligator-orange-6315","probability":0.35},{"player":"rabbit-yellow-6681","probability":0.10}],"behavioral_basis":["buffalo accepted an early trade that left them notably worse off (accepted alligator's G3 for B4) — consistent with Irene's tendency to concede/pay others to produce equitable-looking outcomes and to accept modest offers even at personal cost.","alligator proposed several trades that favored the proposer but also accepted a small-loss offer from rabbit late in the game, matching Irene's willingness to accept modest losses to rebalance payoffs and to avoid risky gambles (many declines).","rabbit's behavior is more self-directed and unsuccessful (many declined proposals) and shows less evidence of the fairness-driven concessions and overpaying that characterize Irene."],"uncertainty_notes":["Small number of observed rounds and proposals — limited sample to infer stable social motives.","Private chip valuations strongly affect whether trades are costly or generous; identical actions may reflect different motives across players.","We cannot observe verbal framing (e.g., explicit appeals to 'even splits') which is a key part of Irene's profile and might distinguish players more clearly." ]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"cat-orange-2462","probability":0.74},{"player":"peacock-red-3668","probability":0.2},{"player":"otter-blue-7516","probability":0.06}],"behavioral_basis":["cat-orange-2462 frequently accepted modest proposals and made proposals that overcompensated partners (e.g., offering more chips than strictly necessary), consistent with a fairness-first, cooperative stance and willingness to rebalance payoffs.","peacock-red-3668 proposed even-count trades (4-for-4) and sometimes overpaid (4 green for 3 red), signaling a preference for equitable-looking splits, though their proposals produced larger personal gains than cat’s.","otter-blue-7516 pursued larger, riskier proposals and declined many offers, consistent with pursuing personal gain (high-value red) rather than prioritizing equal outcomes."],"uncertainty_notes":["Observations cover only three rounds; behavior may shift over more play.","Private chip valuations drive many proposal choices, so some 'fair' offers may reflect value asymmetries rather than intrinsic fairness motives.","Random selection when multiple acceptances occurred can mask true partner preferences and motives."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"badger-red-5032","probability":0.60},{"player":"camel-yellow-5577","probability":0.25},{"player":"squid-yellow-9545","probability":0.15}],"behavioral_basis":["badger repeatedly accepted trades that increased others' payouts as well as his own (accepted camel's red-for-blue and squid's red-for-green trades), consistent with someone willing to concede or accept modest offers to rebalance payoffs.","badger made cautious proposals and declined many offers, matching Irene's tendency to avoid risky gambles and expect cooperation rather than risk-taking bargaining.","camel made many proposals framed as straightforward exchanges (trading less-preferred chips for blue) and benefited once; this partial willingness to trade but frequent declines suggests some cooperative intent but less emphasis on explicit even splits than badger.","squid's proposals consistently asked for high-value blues while offering low-value reds (cheap offers) and were more self-interested and strategic, which contrasts with Irene's fairness-first framing."],"uncertainty_notes":["Behavioral inferences are noisy: many proposals were declined, limiting observed revealed preferences.","Private chip valuations affect what looks like \"fair\" or \"generous\" offers for each player; identical actions can reflect fairness or simple self-interest depending on private values.","Small number of accepted trades (two) means conclusions about fairness orientation are tentative; probabilities reflect these uncertainties."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"dolphin-orange-3122","probability":0.7},{"player":"duck-orange-4971","probability":0.2},{"player":"whale-red-86","probability":0.1}],"behavioral_basis":["Dolphin framed proposals around even/large symmetric quantities (e.g., offering BLUE:10 for RED:10) and repeatedly declined others' offers until a more balanced one appeared — consistent with a fairness-first player who punishes perceived selfishness and accepts modest, equitable deals.","Duck frequently accepted modest gains from others (small positive payoffs of +0.20 and +0.40), which matches Irene's tendency to accept modest offers and concede to rebalance payoffs, but duck's own proposals were self-interested and often declined, so fit is weaker.","Whale consistently proposed self-serving profitable trades that gave only small benefits to partners (maximizing own payoff), which contrasts with Irene's fairness and overpaying tendencies, making whale the least likely match."],"uncertainty_notes":["Small number of rounds and many declined proposals limit confidence in inferred motives.","Players' private chip valuations mean some proposals that look selfish might have been intended as equitable from the proposer's perspective.","Random selection when multiple acceptances and the absence of verbal framing leave room for alternate interpretations of motives."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"camel-red-6481","probability":0.6},{"player":"gorilla-yellow-5964","probability":0.3},{"player":"monkey-yellow-524","probability":0.1}],"behavioral_basis":["camel-red-6481 frequently framed proposals in small, even-count trades (e.g., 2-for-2), used modest offers and accepted reasonable deals — consistent with an actor who prioritizes fairness/equal splits and avoids large risky gambles.","gorilla-yellow-5964 made some even-count proposals and often accepted modest offers, and declined overtly selfish offers — but many of their proposals extracted value for themselves (giving low-value blues to get reds), making them a weaker match than camel.","monkey-yellow-524 consistently pursued personally profitable trades, achieved the largest gains, and proposed larger-value-extracting exchanges — this self-interested pattern is inconsistent with the fairness-first Irene profile."],"uncertainty_notes":["Only a small number of rounds observed — behavioral patterns might change with more data.","Observed proposals are count-based; equal chip counts do not imply equal value transfers because players have private valuations, so fairness-motivated framing can be confounded with opportunistic play.","We cannot observe verbal framing (e.g., explicit appeals to equality), so matching relies on revealed choices and responses which admit ambiguity."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"kangaroo-blue-9562","probability":0.57},{"player":"squid-purple-3630","probability":0.28},{"player":"butterfly-blue-7661","probability":0.15}],"behavioral_basis":["kangaroo-blue-9562 often declined clearly exploitative offers (punishing selfishness) and at least once proposed a trade that gave the partner a larger gain (Round0 Turn1), consistent with a preference for fairness even at some personal cost; kangaroo also avoided the very risky, extreme proposals seen from squid.","squid-purple-3630 shows cooperative tendencies (accepted multiple proposals as responder) and, when proposing (Round0 Turn2), produced a trade that benefited the partner more than the proposer — matching the tendency to overpay partners to appear equitable — but also made at least one clearly risky/selfish proposal (Round2 Turn2), which reduces fit with Irene.","butterfly-blue-7661 accepted several modest offers (consistent with Irene’s willingness to accept small gains and rebalance others), but as proposer repeatedly made very lopsided, high-gain-for-self offers (e.g., offering low-value BLUE to buy lots of GREEN) which conflicts with Irene’s stated preference to frame proposals around even splits and avoid exploitative bargains."],"uncertainty_notes":["Small number of rounds and limited proposal text — we can’t observe verbal framing ('even split') or intent directly.","Players’ private valuations differ and proposals can look generous or selfish from different perspectives; matching is based only on observed payoff changes and accept/decline patterns.","Some behaviors are mixed (players both accept and reject trades, sometimes generous and sometimes selfish), so assignment is probabilistic rather than definitive."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"goose-orange-349","probability":0.55},{"player":"ram-red-1301","probability":0.30},{"player":"butterfly-orange-8262","probability":0.15}],"behavioral_basis":["goose accepted a modest loss early (accepted GREEN:5 for PURPLE:6, -$0.50), which matches Irene’s tendency to accept modest offers and concede to rebalance others’ payoffs.","ram similarly accepted a small loss (accepted RED:6 for GREEN:5, ~-$0.09) and frequently declined unprofitable or risky proposals, consistent with Irene’s avoidance of risky gambles and willingness to tolerate small inequities for cooperation.","butterfly made at least one proposal that overpaid (PURPLE:4 to buy GREEN:6 was slightly negative for them), which is consistent with Irene’s tendency to overpay partners to create equitable-looking outcomes, but overall butterfly’s behavior was more self-interested and often declined trades, reducing the match strength."],"uncertainty_notes":["Limited sample of proposals and no free-form framing text — Irene’s stated propensity to ‘frame proposals around even splits’ cannot be directly observed from numeric offers alone.","Private chip values drive accept/decline behavior; some observed concessions may reflect value asymmetries rather than normative fairness motives.","Random selection when multiple acceptances and small payout changes make it hard to distinguish intentional overpaying for equity from opportunistic or noisy play."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"iguana-red-1430","probability":0.75},{"player":"whale-pink-487","probability":0.2},{"player":"duck-red-8937","probability":0.05}],"behavioral_basis":["Iguana frequently makes loss-making or small-scale offers (e.g. PURPLE:5 for GREEN:3 and BLUE:2 for RED:3) consistent with overpaying to produce fair-looking outcomes and sometimes taking a net loss to benefit partners (ROUND2 TURN0 resulted in iguana -0.30 while duck +0.30). Iguana also accepts modest advantageous offers from others, matching a willingness to accept fair splits.","Whale’s proposals are typically profitable to the proposer (BLUE:8 for RED:8, PURPLE:8 for GREEN:8, PURPLE:3 for RED:3) indicating self-benefit and efficiency orientation rather than sacrifice for equality. Whale sometimes enables others to gain but does not consistently overpay to equalize.","Duck repeatedly proposes highly self-favoring trades (e.g. PURPLE:5 for GREEN:10) and avoids many proposals by others, indicating a more selfish, payoff-maximizing style that punishes offers that aren’t strongly in duck’s favor."],"uncertainty_notes":["Only three rounds of behavior observed — limited sample to infer stable social preferences.","Private chip values are known here but players’ internal fairness framing isn’t observable; similar actions can arise from different motives (e.g. risk aversion vs fairness).","Random selection when multiple accept means some acceptance patterns may reflect attempts to be chosen rather than pure fairness motives."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"whale-pink-7682","probability":0.6},{"player":"swan-green-9081","probability":0.3},{"player":"duck-purple-3491","probability":0.1}],"behavioral_basis":["Accepted a clearly disadvantageous trade (round 0) — consistent with conceding own payoff to rebalance others' outcomes and prioritize fairness.","Tended to accept modest/gentle gains when offered (accepted swan’s small beneficial offer) rather than hold out for large personal gains — matches willingness to accept modest offers.","Proposals and later cautious play produced many declined trades, consistent with avoiding risky or aggressive gambles; overall behavior appears more other-regarding than the overtly self-interested proposer (duck)."],"uncertainty_notes":["We infer motives from limited moves; some actions (e.g., accepting a bad deal) could reflect errors or confusion rather than deliberate fairness.","Private chip valuations mean some proposals that look selfish may have been framed as fair to that proposer; proposers generally gained from their offers, so matching is based mostly on responder behavior.","Irene’s description emphasizes proposers overpaying to equalize; observed players rarely overpaid as proposers, so the match relies more on responder concessions and aversion to risky gambles."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"butterfly-yellow-2785","probability":0.65},{"player":"cat-pink-5829","probability":0.25},{"player":"badger-blue-8654","probability":0.10}],"behavioral_basis":["Accepted modest, low-profit offers (e.g., accepted cat's RED:3 for BLUE:3 yielding only +$0.30), consistent with a willingness to forego larger gains for fairer outcomes.","Ended the game with the lowest total payout, suggesting a tendency to prioritize equitable or psychologically fair trades over personal earnings.","Made multiple proposals that look oriented toward balanced exchanges (including an equal 5-for-5 trade) and frequently had proposals declined rather than pushing exploitative deals — consistent with an equality-first framing."],"uncertainty_notes":["No proposers in the observed data clearly sacrificed positive expected payoff to create equal outcomes, so the match rests on subtle signals (modest acceptances, lower final earnings) rather than explicit 'overpaying' behavior.","Players' private valuations can make identical quantity proposals look fair to one player but exploitative to another; inferred motives may reflect strategic constraints rather than pure fairness preferences.","Behavioral overlap: cat also accepted modest offers and made equal-count proposals, and badger often used equal-count trades too (but with larger self gains), so ranking among these three is somewhat uncertain."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"dog-purple-778","probability":0.78},{"player":"owl-orange-1045","probability":0.15},{"player":"peacock-purple-2249","probability":0.07}],"behavioral_basis":["Dog (dog-purple-778) repeatedly made tight, self-favoring proposals that nevertheless raised total surplus (e.g., offering GREEN to buy multiple REDs and producing net gains for both sides in R1 and R2). That matches Marcus’s pattern of designing efficient trades that increase his own expected return and using assertive offers.","Owl (owl-orange-1045) often accepted profitable offers for himself and benefited from dog’s proposals, consistent with opportunistic acceptance of surplus-creating deals but not with the repeated proposer-initiated, optimization-driven pattern Marcus favors.","Peacock (peacock-purple-2249) proposed more generous deals (e.g., GREEN 5 for RED 3) and sometimes sacrificed personal payoff slightly, which is less consistent with Marcus’s tendency to minimize concessions."],"uncertainty_notes":["Small number of rounds/proposals limits confidence in inferred long-run style.","Random selection when multiple players accept can make observed payoffs differ from intended expected returns, obscuring motives behind some acceptances (e.g., dog’s acceptance of a slightly disadvantageous trade).","We observe actions and payoffs but not private deliberations; some deviations could be mistakes, risk-taking, or strategic signaling rather than core preference."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"ram-red-5745","probability":0.78},{"player":"iguana-orange-9224","probability":0.16},{"player":"dolphin-purple-6605","probability":0.06}],"behavioral_basis":["Ram consistently proposed trades that moved low-value red from Iguana to himself, increasing total surplus while also increasing his own payoff — matches a planner who seeks efficient, self-benefiting trades.","Ram’s offers were tight and escalated across rounds (1G for 2R, then 2G for 4R), showing assertive, optimization-minded bargaining and willingness to risk rejection to improve expected return.","Iguana sometimes behaved efficiently (accepting beneficial offers from Ram, proposing trades to obtain green) but also accepted a clearly negative trade late — partially consistent with efficiency focus but less reliably so.","Dolphin’s pattern (mostly declining, opportunistic acceptance of a harmful trade for Iguana, and unsuccessful proposals) looks less like the cold, efficiency-first strategist profile. "],"uncertainty_notes":["Limited observation window (3 rounds) may miss other strategic behavior or planning not shown here.","Responses are probabilistic when multiple acceptors exist and private valuations are known only to players; some actions could reflect risk preferences or mistakes rather than pure strategy.","Similar public valuations for Ram and Dolphin (both value red at 0.50) make some behaviors hard to distinguish; assignment leans on observed proposal patterns and payoff changes."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"goose-purple-9985","probability":0.72},{"player":"buffalo-orange-6610","probability":0.23},{"player":"monkey-pink-6874","probability":0.05}],"behavioral_basis":["goose repeatedly made tight, efficiency-improving offers (e.g., 2 GREEN for 2 RED) that increased both proposer and partner payoffs and moved high‑value RED toward the high‑value holder—consistent with an optimizer who crafts trades that raise total surplus while improving own return.","buffalo made consistent, profitable proposals as well (e.g., 2 RED for 3 GREEN) that appear opportunistic and aimed at extracting modest gains without big concessions—somewhat aligned with an efficiency-first trader but less clearly the planner/optimizer archetype.","monkey consistently accepted profitable incoming offers but made proposals that were strongly unfavorable to themselves (large asks/offers that were declined), indicating either inattention or a non-optimizer; this behavior is not consistent with Marcus’s tight, calculated proposer style."],"uncertainty_notes":["Only three rounds with a small number of proposals limit confidence in inferred motives; a single anomalous proposal (goose’s round‑2 RED-for-GREEN move that reduced their payoff) could reflect noise or a hidden strategic motive.","Random selection when multiple acceptances occurred and private valuations mean some acceptance/decline choices may reflect risk tolerance or calculation errors rather than stable preference structure.","Players may be experimenting early in the game; longer play or private chat would reduce ambiguity."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"otter-pink-5988","probability":0.76},{"player":"squid-yellow-4597","probability":0.18},{"player":"ram-pink-8623","probability":0.06}],"behavioral_basis":["otter repeatedly proposed trades that clearly increased both his and his partners' payoffs (e.g., 5 GREEN for 5 RED), showing an optimizer’s eye for mutually beneficial, efficient exchanges and targeted partners who would accept — matches Marcus’s efficiency-first, assertive trading style.","otter’s proposals were tight and repeated across rounds, indicating planning and exploitation of the rules to secure gains without large concessions, consistent with Marcus’s playbook.","squid made at least one clearly extractive proposal (RED 4 for GREEN 10) that increased his own payoff while reducing another’s — manipulative and self-serving, but not focused on group surplus, so somewhat similar to Marcus’s willingness to use rules, but diverges on efficiency/fairness.","ram frequently accepted or made offers that reduced his own payoff (and sometimes suffered large losses), which conflicts with Marcus’s stated tendency to avoid unprofitable concessions and to optimize expected return."],"uncertainty_notes":["Inference is based on a small number of rounds and observed proposals/responses; actual private reasoning or off-record intentions are unknown.","Player behavior may reflect misunderstandings, risk preferences, or experimentation rather than stable strategy — this can bias match estimates.","Roles and turn order may shape proposals (e.g., proposers may behave differently than responders), so similarity to Marcus could be under- or over-estimated depending on unseen dynamics."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"camel-pink-767","probability":0.75},{"player":"butterfly-yellow-2139","probability":0.20},{"player":"alligator-orange-9612","probability":0.05}],"behavioral_basis":["camel-pink-767: Initiated surplus-increasing trades that raised group payoff while capturing a larger share for the proposer (Round0 Turn0). Offers were compact and exploit comparative advantage (she values RED more than others), consistent with optimizing behavior and tight, assertive proposals.","butterfly-yellow-2139: Made at least one large, mutually beneficial offer (Round0 Turn1) that boosted total surplus substantially and improved her payoff — indicates attention to efficiency and personal gain, but later proposals were extreme/unrealistic and often rejected, less consistent with careful optimization.","alligator-orange-9612: Accepted a profitable, but small-gain trade initially, then repeatedly proposed and received declined offers — behavior appears less strategic/planned and more error-prone or opportunistic than the described profile."],"uncertainty_notes":["Observations span only three rounds and a few proposals; limited data can make behavioral inferences noisy.","Private valuations drive a lot of the observed actions; some proposals (especially later ones) might reflect changing holdings or experimental/random play rather than stable strategy.","Camel-pink-767 made a few later proposals that were declined and seem less consistent with the early optimizing move; this introduces some uncertainty in the strength of the match."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"iguana-blue-1059","probability":0.82},{"player":"owl-green-168","probability":0.14},{"player":"squid-purple-9957","probability":0.04}],"behavioral_basis":["Iguana repeatedly proposed trades that exchanged low-value red for higher-value green, producing clear personal gains while leaving only a small surplus to the partner — matches Marcus’s tendency to design efficient, tight offers that raise total payoff without large concessions.","Owl consistently accepted offers that produced positive payoff for themselves (including very large gains from squid’s proposals) but made ineffective proposals that rarely attracted partners — consistent with opportunistic acceptance rather than proposer-driven optimization.","Squid proposed trades that exchanged large amounts of low-value red to acquire modest gains in blue/green but often left big gains to the counterparty, suggesting more generous or noisy proposals rather than the tight, efficiency-focused bargaining Marcus prefers."],"uncertainty_notes":["Only three rounds of play — limited data to infer long-run planning or risk tolerance precisely.","Players’ private values drive which offers look ‘tight’ or ‘generous’; interpretations depend on those known values but internal strategy and intent remain unobserved.","Random selection when multiple acceptors exist and possible off-path considerations (e.g., signaling) could affect observed behavior beyond pure payoff optimization."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"alligator-orange-6315","probability":0.7},{"player":"rabbit-yellow-6681","probability":0.25},{"player":"buffalo-purple-4069","probability":0.05}],"behavioral_basis":["alligator-orange-6315 repeatedly made tight, high–expected-value proposals (e.g., GREEN 3 for BLUE 4 and BLUE 2 for RED 3) that increased his own payout when accepted — consistent with an optimization-focused, assertive proposer who minimizes concessions.","rabbit-yellow-6681 consistently proposed small positive-EV trades and declined others, showing a focus on preserving personal expected return and cautious, planned offers (fits the ‘optimize EV without sacrificing self-interest’ aspect).","buffalo-purple-4069’s acceptance of a clearly negative trade and otherwise unaccepted proposals is not consistent with the cold, optimization-first profile — suggests mistakes or non-optimizer behavior."],"uncertainty_notes":["Small number of rounds/trades limits confidence in inferred strategy.","Two players share identical private valuations (alligator and buffalo), making behavioral distinctions noisier.","Some actions (e.g., accepting a sure loss) are anomalous and could reflect errors, misunderstanding, or unobserved motives rather than stable preferences."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"goose-green-3433","probability":0.65},{"player":"buffalo-green-2230","probability":0.25},{"player":"bear-green-5745","probability":0.10}],"behavioral_basis":["Goose consistently proposed tight, efficiency-improving swaps to get higher-value RED (5-for-5, 4-for-4, etc.), accepted only personally profitable trades, and behaved like an optimizer maximizing own payoff — matches Marcus’s assertive, efficiency-first style.","Buffalo repeatedly proposed trades that increase their own expected value to obtain RED (offering GREEN for RED), showed persistence across rounds and willingness to adjust generosity — aligns with Marcus’s tolerance for calculated risk and planning, though offers were slightly more conciliatory than Marcus’s typical tight offers.","Bear made some opportunistic proposer moves but also showed indifference (equal private values) and accepted neutral trades; behavior is less clearly optimization-driven and more passive than Marcus’s profile."],"uncertainty_notes":["Only three rounds of play; many proposals were declined so revealed preferences are limited.","Accept/decline choices can mask motives (e.g., acceptance when indifferent or to avoid randomness), so inferred intent may be noisy.","Players’ private value heterogeneity makes some proposer choices look similar across types (all who value RED more will seek RED), reducing discrimination power."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"camel-yellow-5577","probability":0.6},{"player":"squid-yellow-9545","probability":0.3},{"player":"badger-red-5032","probability":0.1}],"behavioral_basis":["Camel repeatedly proposed tight, self-favoring swaps (RED for BLUE) that raised his own payoff and in at least one case increased total group payout — consistent with designing efficient trades that improve his return.","Squid made similarly aggressive, optimization-style offers (trading low-value RED for high-value BLUE/GREEN) and executed a mutually beneficial RED→GREEN trade, matching Marcus’s focus on trades that raise surplus if they increase his payoff.","Badger proposed deals that often improved only his own payoff even when they reduced or did not raise group surplus (e.g., GREEN for BLUE), which is less aligned with Marcus’s explicit preference for raising group surplus when feasible."],"uncertainty_notes":["Only a small number of proposals were executed; many offers were declined, making inferred intentions noisy.","Random selection when multiple acceptances occur and private-information incentives can obscure whether proposals were designed to increase total surplus or to exploit specific partners.","Players’ off-record strategic considerations (e.g., signaling, risk aversion, or expectations about future rounds) could explain some proposals and responses in ways that mimic or diverge from Marcus’s optimization-driven behavior."]}
